data.title: Constellation Brands: Turnaround In Play - But Patience Required
data.description:
Constellation Brands (STZ) is an international beer, wine, and spirit producer that has experienced a 41% decline in the stock price over the past 12 months. The company presents a defensive approach, dividend income, and above-market margins, which may help withstand market turbulence.

STZ undergoes significant top- and bottom-line growth headwinds, but the expansion is expected to resume in FY2026. Berkshire Hathaway owns 7.70% of the company, and that may appear reassuring for long-term investors. Constellation Brands trades at a forward P/E of 12, underlining a 26% discount versus the sector median of 16x.

Although I think it may complete its bottoming phase, I favor a conservative approach. I believe it may take time for the market to rethink its thesis about the company, and I argue it is likely to deliver market-like performance. I rate Constellation Brands a hold with a $150 price target over the next 12 months. This presents a 9% upside possibility without dividends, but I am not sure whether STZ is well-positioned to significantly outperform the benchmark.

FQ2 2026: Double-Beat Clouds Double-Digit Business Decline

On October 6, the company delivered FQ2 2026 results. Although this was a double-beat performance, it underscored the decline in top- and bottom-line results. The market reacted to the report with a 2.45% decline on the earnings day.

Constellation Brands posted $2.48 billion in revenue, highlighting a 15% decline on a year-over-year basis, but $23 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $3.63 in diluted EPS, a 19% drop over the past 12 months, but $0.22 above analysts' estimates.

In the earnings report, management highlighted that the beer business remained the largest top-line contributor. It has generated $2.34 billion in revenue, accounting for 94% of the total revenue. The segment has declined by 8%, and that appears to be concerning. In addition to this, the company's performance remains heavily dependent on the beer business, and if the trend continues, the sentiment may remain clouded.

The wine and spirit business posted $136 million in revenue, a substantial contraction from $389 million in the same quarter last year. This highlights a more structural issue STZ may be facing, potentially tied to consumer habits and people's preferences. Moreover, management highlighted that it has experienced a 76% drop in shipments, mainly driven by the sharp decline.

Furthermore, the beer segment has also experienced a decline in shipments, reflecting a 9% drop on a year-over-year basis. I think investors should monitor whether the company can turn around the trend. If it continues, it may heavily contribute to the bearish thesis, and this also supports my neutral stance. I favor seeing an improvement, and until then, it may experience further downward momentum.

On the downside, Constellation Brands continues experiencing a decline in operating margins, and I think that is another significant headwind. For the beer segment, the operating margin has dropped from 42.6% to 40.6% over the past 12 months. The wine and spirits business experienced a negative 14.6% operating margin versus 18.1% in the same quarter last year. This heavily clouds the bullish narrative and supports continued consolidation.

Management guided for the remainder of the year. The company anticipates a continuation of the business decline, highlighting a 5% drop in organic sales on a year-over-year basis. The beer segment is anticipated to drop by 3%, while the wine and spirit category is expected to experience another double-digit decline. I believe this doesn't signal a turnaround story yet, and it supports my Hold rating.

The consumer staples company is trading at a suppressed valuation, but based on the quarterly performance, there are reasons behind the weak investor sentiment. The business seems to be in decline, and there are no clear signals that the company's performance may pick up from here.

I think that dividend income, a defensive approach, may argue for bottoming around these levels, but I don't think STZ is well-positioned to outperform the benchmark in the short-term future. It will be interesting to see whether performance continues to erode, and if that's the case, the stock may continue its downward trend.

Undervalued For A Reason, Severe Headwinds Remain

Constellation Brands is trading at a forward P/E of 12, a 26% discount versus peers at 16x, and approximately 92% undervaluation versus the average company in the S&P500 at 23x earnings multiple. One could argue that STZ may rebound from here, but I think if it does, it is still likely to underperform the benchmark.

On the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 3.84 and 2.65, respectively, the consumer staples name seems to be trading at a 2.5x premium at the midpoint versus the sector medians of 1.45 and 1.07. This emphasizes a few key things. This signals that the market anticipates resumed revenue growth, and if results come in weaker-than-anticipated, the downward trend may proceed further. This also highlights that STZ isn't as cheap as it may appear to be, which supports my neutral stance.

Constellation Brands has a heavily leveraged capital structure, and that is another argument for a clouded outlook. STZ has $10.54 billion in total obligations, which remains elevated, and $72 million in cash seems to be insignificant. This results in a meaningful financial burden, indicating $390 million in interest expenses over the past 12 months. This weighs on the bottom-line growth projection and validates a neutral position.

Over the past 12 months, STZ experienced a 6% decline in revenue, a significant disadvantage versus peers at 3% growth. As highlighted above, this is mainly due to a significant decline in the wine and spirit business. On the upside, it may become less impactful over the following quarters, and if the beer segment revenue growth accelerates, the top-line performance may rebound from here. On the downside, due to this, STZ becomes a less diversified business and more dependent on a single segment, which may cap the upside and discount the premium.

Constellation Brands operates at premium, above-industry margins, and that is supporting the bottoming thesis at the current price tag. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 52% appears to reflect a 45% advantage versus peers at 36%. The net income margin (TTM) of 13% highlights a 2.9x outperformance versus peers at 4.38%. If margin erosion is sustained, and STZ continues operating at these profitability metrics, this may support further profitability that could lead to gradual improvement in fundamentals.

To sum up, there are reasons behind why the stock appears to be cheap. Although it operates at premium margins, capital structure remains heavily leveraged. Top- and bottom-line headwinds cloud the outlook, and until the fundamentals improve, I think it may continue performing at a suppressed earnings multiple. The stock offers a defensive approach, but it may be discounted due to a weakening business. Nevertheless, I think it may bottom at these levels, but it will take time for both fundamentals and market sentiment to improve; thus, likely to underperform the benchmark.

Wall Street indicates $11.49 in diluted EPS for fiscal FY2026, and $12.41 in diluted EPS for fiscal FY2027. This suggests it may get worse before it gets better, and I generally agree with such a forecast. I think the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line growth; therefore, if we apply the current earnings multiple of 12x to the fiscal FY2027 EPS of $12.41, we arrive at my price target of $150. I think this implies a reasonable forecast, highlighting that it may take time for the market to rethink the thesis about the company.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Constellation Brands that could significantly impact stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, the business seems to be in decline, and there are no signs of performance turning around. If STZ continues delivering top- and bottom-line erosion, the downward momentum may continue further. Although the wine and spirit business will become less impactful on the performance, management guides for a 3% net sales decline for the beer segment in FY2026, too. If the trend continues, it may be challenging to justify multiple expansions.

The consumer staples company operates at premium margins, but also experiences erosion. If profitability metrics continue to decline, the market may not be willing to pay a premium for the company's stock, and the turnaround story may remain clouded. In addition to this, US consumer spending weakens, and alcohol remains a non-core item. If, for instance, inflation persists and the Fed decides to pause the easing cycle, consumers may remain pressured, and that could negatively affect the company's performance.

There is also a demographic risk tied to consumer habits and preferences. Although overall alcohol consumption has risen in the US, it seems that Gen Z is drinking way less. I think people are becoming more aware of their health, and seem to be favoring a healthier way of living. In the long term, this could lead to a significant risk if the trend continues.

On the other hand, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, the company's performance may improve, and the stock could rebound from these levels. However, management needs to execute and turn the current trend around, and if that happens, STZ may bottom at the current levels.

Conclusion: Constellation Brands Is A Hold

Although STZ has significant top- and bottom-line headwinds, given its significant sell-off, it may appear that the majority of the concerns could already be priced in the stock price. The company continues operating at premium margins, offers a defensive approach, and has BRK's backing by investment, which may position it as a compelling opportunity.

However, management guides for continued decline in business performance, and that makes me concerned. The company is also experiencing headwinds tied to consumer habit changes and a healthier way of living, which could cap the premium. Furthermore, margins appear to be eroding, and if the trend continues, investor confidence may remain clouded.

I think the company's stock price may appreciate following bottom-line growth, but management needs to execute well to turn the performance around. Therefore, I rate Constellation Brands a Hold with a $150 price target over the next 12 months, presenting a 9% upside possibility, but I don't think the company is well-positioned to outperform the benchmark. 25 Wall Street analysts indicate a $171 price target over the next year, highlighting a 24% upside potential. I favor a more conservative approach, but if top- and bottom-line growth resumes, this is definitely a possible outcome.

data.title: Japanese Bonds: A 2026 Wild Card
data.description:
Happy December! It’s the last month of 2025, which has been a strange year in oh so many ways. It is also the month when, by custom and tradition, our leading financial institutions tell us, in very specific terms, how the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq and the Dow will all fare in 2026. Please.

You will have just as much insight into what the US stock market will look like twelve months hence if you ask the question of your friendly neighborhood psychic. Or your dog, for that matter.

Rather than trying to pin a number on something that is entirely unknowable, let us instead think about what some of the wild cards might be that buffet assets hither and yon next year. One that we have been paying attention to recently is the Japanese bond market.

It was not too long ago – little more than a year in fact – when the most notable feature of this market was negative yields. Under the hyper-stimulatory policies of the Bank of Japan, investors in Japanese government bonds (JGBs) not only received no interest income, but those interest payments actually went in the other direction

Takaichi-nomics

Those days are gone. The 10-year Japanese government bond yield has nearly doubled since the beginning of the year, with much of the growth spurt coming in the past two months as the new government led by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi came into office with big plans for some major fiscal stimulus to shake Japan’s economy out of its recent lethargy.

At the same time, the Japanese yen, which had risen sharply against the US dollar earlier in the year, has plunged by around seven percent against the greenback.

The Bank of Japan has already raised rates three times since March 2024, at which time the benchmark policy rate was still negative. The BoJ meets again next Thursday (one day after the Fed holds its December meeting), and analysts are now expecting that this meeting will produce another rate hike. BoJ Governor Kazuo Ueda suggested as much in public comments earlier this week, and swaps markets are now pricing in around a 70 percent probability of another hike.

The policy action stems from concerns that the new Takaichi government will be tapping the debt market for sizable supplemental issuances next year in order to fund a massive budgetary program to promote economic growth.

Investors have grown wary of longer-dated bonds recently, with a collective holding of breath and then a measure of relief when a 20-year JGB auction this week managed to scrape out enough demand to cover the issue.

Observers worry that the government’s plans will prove too ambitious for the market to swallow, particularly if the size of the government’s budget necessitates going back to those shaky longer maturities. The same concerns have been behind the recent pronounced weakness in the yen against other major currencies.

Carry That Weight

The other major implication for bond markets outside Japan is the way higher yields upend the carry trade, a long-standing and arguably stabilizing factor in global credit markets. In a world of ultra-cheap Japanese yields, investors borrowed in yen and invested in higher-yielding opportunities elsewhere.

Japanese financial institutions are among the world’s largest creditors, and in recent years Japan surpassed China as the largest international holder of US Treasuries.

But the prospect of higher rates in their home country may drive the Japanese financial institutions to repatriate a meaningful part of their overseas holdings, which in turn could drive rates higher elsewhere. Including, yes, the US Treasury market. And equity markets, as we all know, are downstream from and highly affected by changes in fixed income yields.

This could be one catalyst for a very messy year in global credit. There are plenty of other reasons to not be looking forward to bond markets next year. Here at home, latter-day bond vigilantes have been trying to steer the White House away from turning the Fed into a supplicant to the president’s monomaniacal desire for ultra-low interest rates.

Dissension among Federal Open Market Committee members has been a factor of the past several FOMC meetings, a trend which could turn even sharper in the months to come.

But even in the event that the administration were to get its way and start pushing the Fed funds rate ever lower, the forces affecting longer-term rates – i.e., the ones that have more impact on real-world economic activity – are much less easy for politicians to control.

Such as, for example, a sizable exit by Japanese banks from their US Treasury holdings in response to the higher yields back home. This may not come to pass, of course. But it will require close attention in the weeks and months ahead.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HCA Healthcare Is Caring For Patients And Investors Alike
data.description:
It’s rare to find a company that benefits society and investors. Our ability to screen the entire U.S. stock market for the best and worst stocks based on criteria proven to generate alpha makes us uniquely qualified to find such rare companies.

Our Long Idea is a company that has consistently grown profits for over a decade, in good and bad times.

Even though the company’s stock has significantly outperformed the S&P 500 year-to-date, it remains undervalued and continues to provide strong upside potential.

We previously made HCA Healthcare Inc. (HCA) a Long Idea in December 2018, and have reiterated our bullish view on the stock multiple times since.

After a strong third quarter, in which the company beat top- and bottom-line estimates and raised guidance for the full fiscal year, HCA is up 55%+ since our last update published in February 2025.

Even after significantly outperforming, the stock remains undervalued, in large part due to HCA Healthcare’s growing profits and cash flows. Our thesis for HCA Healthcare remains firmly intact, and its stock continues to provide strong upside potential.

HCA offers favorable Risk/Reward based on the company’s:

  • position to provide care to an aging population,
  • rising admissions and occupancy rates,
  • increasing hospital and bed counts,
  • leading market share, cash flow, and profitability, and
  • cheap stock price that implies profits will never grow again from current levels.

What’s Working

As the largest healthcare system in the U.S., with over 190 hospitals and 2,400 ambulatory sites, the company is well-positioned to continue growing profits as the population gets older.

We recently highlighted in our Long Idea report on Universal Health Services (UHS) that two key trends will drive the growth of healthcare systems in the U.S.

1) The Aging U.S. Population

The U.S. population age 65 and older increased 3.1% year-over-year (YoY) in 2024, while the population under the age of 18 decreased by 0.2% YoY. While children still outnumber adults in the U.S., the gap is narrowing. The share of the population age 65 and older has increased from 12% in 2004 to 18% in 2024, while the share of children declined from 25% to 22%.

This trend will persist. The Congressional Budget Office projects the ratio of people ages 25-64 to people ages 65+ to decline from 2.8 to 1 in 2025 to 2.2 to 1 by 2055. Figure 1 illustrates how this ratio has steadily declined since the 1950s.

The Census Bureau expects the oldest of the U.S. population, the centenarian (ages 100 and older) population, to quadruple from 2024 to 2054.

America’s senior population is rising while its youth population is shrinking.

Figure 1: The Ratio of 25-64 Year Olds to 65+ Year Olds: 1950 Through 2055

2) Healthcare Spending Increases with Age

The link between aging and rising healthcare spending is strong: older patients generally require more medical services and spend more on such services.

Accordingly, the latest Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (cms.gov) data shows that the per capita spending of a person aged 85 or older is 8.5x higher than the spending of a child aged 18 or under. For those aged 65-84, healthcare spending is 4.9x higher than for those aged <18. See Figure 2.

Figure 2: Healthcare Spending Per Person by Age Group in the U.S.

Acute Care Services Play a Key Role

General, acute care hospitals typically provide a full range of services to accommodate multiple medical specialties, such as internal medicine, general surgery, cardiology, oncology, neurosurgery, orthopedics, and obstetrics, as well as diagnostic and emergency services.

Age, inevitably, brings a wide range of health problems, and older patients will increasingly rely on the comprehensive services and treatments available at acute care hospitals, which drives demand for acute care hospitals and services over the next decade.

Precedence Research forecasts the global acute hospital care market to grow from $3.6 trillion in 2025 to $6.2 trillion in 2034, or 6% compounded annually. See Figure 3.

Figure 3: Acute Hospital Care Market Forecast: 2025 Through 2034

Well-Positioned to Serve the Population

HCA Healthcare’s portfolio is heavily focused on its general, acute care hospitals. At the end of 2024, of the 190 hospitals under its control, 180 were general, acute care hospitals, 6 were behavioral care hospitals, and 4 were rehabilitation hospitals.

HCA Healthcare’s focus on general acute care services positions it to meet the growing healthcare needs, as evidenced by the growth in admissions and occupancy rates.

HCA Healthcare’s equivalent admissions, which provide a general measure of combined inpatient and outpatient volume, rose 3% compounded annually from 3.0 million in 2014 to 4.1 million in the TTM ended 3Q25. See Figure 4.

Similarly, the company’s occupancy rate, which is the percentage of hospital beds in service that are occupied by patients, increased from 55% in 2014 to 74% in the first nine months of 2025.

Figure 4: HCA Healthcare’s Equivalent Admissions: 2014 – TTM ended 3Q25

Capacity Expansion Supports Growth

HCA Healthcare has consistently grown its hospital and bed count for over a decade, which allows the company to provide services to a larger population and treat more patients at one time. This expansion also helps HCA Healthcare maintain its position as the largest healthcare system in the U.S., both by hospital count and licensed bed count.

The company has grown its hospital count from 166 in 2014 to 191 at the end of 3Q25, while it increased its licensed bed count from over 43,000 to over 50,500 over the same time.

Figure 5: HCA Healthcare’s Hospital and Licensed Bed Count: 2014 – End of 3Q25

Expansion Continues

HCA Healthcare already has new hospital and facility plans in place to continue the expansion noted above. Recent projects include:

  • a new 68-bed hospital in Clarksville, Tennessee;
  • a project to turn the Florida Gainesville emergency room into a full-service 90-bed hospital;
  • an expansion to the Good Samaritan Hospital in San Jose, California, which will add 234 beds;
  • an expansion to the Florida Palms West Hospital, which will add 42 beds;
  • an expansion to the Florida West Marion Hospital, which added 40 beds;
  • a new freestanding emergency room in Ocala, Florida, which will add 36 beds, and
  • more committed projects taking place over the next few years.

Proven Quality Fundamentals

HCA Healthcare operates in an industry with growth tailwinds throughout all economic cycles. Management has successfully converted such steady and rising demand into revenue and profit growth across the years.

The company has grown revenue and net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) by 6% and 7% compounded annually since 2007, respectively. See Figure 6.

Additionally, the company’s core earnings grew 14% compounded annually from $598 million in 2007 to $6.5 billion in the TTM ended 3Q25.

More recently, HCA Healthcare improved its NOPAT margin from 11.6% in 2019 to 12.5% in the TTM, while its invested capital turns increased from 1.4 to 1.5 over the same time. Rising NOPAT margin and invested capital turns drive the company’s return on invested capital (ROIC) from 16% in 2019 to 19% in the TTM.

Figure 6: HCA Healthcare’s Revenue and NOPAT: 2007 – TTM ended 3Q25

Industry Leader Through and Through

HCA Healthcare is not only the largest healthcare system in the U.S. by revenue and hospital count, but also the most profitable one by ROIC. In other words, through high operational and capital efficiency, HCA Healthcare leads in both scale and profitability.

Per Figure 7, HCA Healthcare’s ROIC and invested capital turns are higher than all other healthcare system companies, which include fellow Long Idea Universal Health Services, Encompass Health (EHC), Tenet Healthcare (THC), Community Health Systems (CYH), and Select Medical Holding (SEM).

Figure 7: HCA Healthcare’s Profitability Vs. Peers: Trailing Twelve Months (TTM)

Significant Cash Flow Generation

HCA Healthcare has consistently generated large cash flows for over a decade. The company hasn’t recorded a negative free cash flow (FCF) in any fiscal year in our model, which dates to 2008.

The company generated a cumulative $50.4 billion (25% of enterprise value) in FCF from 2014 through 3Q25. See Figure 5. Over the TTM alone, HCA Healthcare generated $10.5 billion in FCF.

Figure 8: HCA Healthcare’s Cumulative Free Cash Flow Since 2014

Potential for 3.7%+ Yield

Since 2018, HCA Healthcare has paid $4.3 billion (4% of market cap) in cumulative dividends and has increased its quarterly dividends from $0.35/share in 1Q18 to $0.72/share in 4Q25. The company’s current dividend, when annualized, provides a 0.6% yield.

HCA also returns capital to shareholders through share repurchases. From 2018 through 3Q25, the company repurchased $35.6 billion (34% of market cap) of shares. In the first nine months of 2025 alone, the company repurchased $7.5 billion of shares.

In January 2025, HCA Healthcare’s board of directors authorized a new $10 billion share repurchase program. As of September 30, 2025, HCA Healthcare has $3.3 billion of shares remaining under its existing repurchase authorization.

Should the company repurchase shares at its TTM repurchase rate, it would deplete its remaining repurchase authorization, which equals 3.1% of the current market cap.

When combined, the dividend and share repurchase yield could reach 3.7%.

It’s worth noting that HCA Healthcare is repurchasing slightly more shares than its FCF would allow. Since 2018, the company has repurchased shares and paid dividends worth $39.9 billion. Over the same time, the company generated $39.4 billion in FCF.

Shares Outstanding on the Decline

HCA Healthcare’s repurchases have meaningfully reduced its shares outstanding from 347 million in 2018 to 243 million at the end of 3Q25. See Figure 9.

We like companies that choose to return capital to shareholders instead of spending it on costly acquisitions or executive bonuses that rarely drive shareholder value creation. Companies that sport strong enough cash flows to consistently lower their shares outstanding, like HCA Healthcare, offer excellent value.

Figure 9: HCA Healthcare’s Shares Outstanding: 2018 – 3Q25

What’s Not Working

Labor Costs Will Remain a Challenge Across the Industry

The shortage of healthcare workers has been a concern across the healthcare industry over the last couple of years. McKinsey projects the global healthcare worker shortage to reach 10 million by 2030.

In the U.S., the American Hospital Association (AHA) projects a nurse assistant shortage of 73,000 and a critical health care worker shortage of 100,000 by 2028, despite projections for a slight registered nurse surplus over the same time.

With ongoing shortages, the labor costs remain a challenge for the entire healthcare industry. However, the most profitable companies, namely HCA Healthcare and Universal Health Services, have proven their ability to effectively manage costs while maintaining profit growth.

HCA Healthcare’s salaries and benefits expense as a percentage of revenue fell from 46% in 2020 to 44% in the TTM ending 3Q25.

Even if the labor costs were to rise again, any deterioration in HCA Healthcare’s profit margin is already priced into its current stock price, as we’ll show below.

Current Price Implies No Profit Growth

At its current price, HCA’s price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio is 1.0. This ratio means the market expects the company’s NOPAT to never grow from TTM levels. Such an expectation looks overly pessimistic given that the company has grown NOPAT by 8% compounded annually over both the past five and ten years.

Below, we use our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to quantify the cash flow expectations for different stock price scenarios for HCA.

In the first scenario, we quantify the expectations baked into the current price. If we assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 9.5% (below TTM margin of 12.5% and 10-year average of 11.6%) from 2025 to 2034,
  • revenue grows at consensus estimates in 2025 (7%) and 2026 (5%), and
  • revenue grows 4% (below 5- and 10-year CAGR of 7%) each year thereafter through 2034, then;

the stock would be worth $475/share today – nearly equal to the current stock price. In this scenario, HCA Healthcare’s NOPAT would grow just 2% compounded annually from 2025 – 2034. For reference, the company has grown NOPAT 7% compounded annually since 2007 (earliest data available).

Shares Could Go 40%+ Higher at Consensus Growth

If we instead assume HCA Healthcare’s:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 12.0% (still below TTM margin of 12.5%) through 2034,
  • revenue grows at consensus estimates in 2025 (7%) and 2026 (5%), and
  • revenue grows 4% (below 5- and 10-year CAGR of 7%) each year thereafter through 2034, then;

the stock would be worth $660/share today—a 40% upside to the current price. In this scenario, HCA Healthcare’s NOPAT would grow just 4% compounded annually through 2034. Should the company’s NOPAT grow more in line with historical levels, the stock has even more upside.

Figure 10 compares HCA Healthcare’s implied future NOPAT in these scenarios to its historical NOPAT.

Figure 10: HCA’s Historical and Implied NOPAT: DCF Valuation Scenarios

data.title: Swiss Water: Premium Process, Questionable Moat And A Shaky Coffee Market
data.description:
Swiss Water Decaffeinated Coffee (TSX:SWP) uses a decaffeinating method that removes the caffeine without any use of chemicals like methylene chloride or ethyl acetate, which are chemical solvents to remove the caffeine from the beans, but the problem is that there is a possibility that it leaves a small amount of chemicals in the beans, which is not desirable to any consumer. No one wants to ingest chemicals from their daily habits like drinking coffee, as there has been a rise in the demand for decaf coffee as individuals get more and more conscious of their health habits, as ingesting chemicals is obviously one of the last things individuals would consciously do. Swiss Water essentially performs a more premium process for roasters and importers to remove the caffeine without leaving a trace of chemicals, which is the main differentiation point between Swiss Water and the traditional/standard practice of decaffeinating coffee. For example, Starbucks still does the direct contact method, and though there is meant to be absolutely 0 presence of methylene chloride since it is toxic, the possibility of small traces or even the possibility of intaking the chemical due to some error is worrying to consumers.

Even though the tariffs for green coffee beans have been dropped with the world's largest exporter of coffee, Brazil, coffee prices have been trending upwards and remain volatile, which does affect Swiss Water indirectly (affects their customers, which ripples onto them). With extremely low volumes and low valuations, potentially accelerating revenues and a shift towards Swiss Water's process rather than traditional ones are bullish for Swiss Water. They also have the existing relationships with large chains like Starbucks to capitalize on if there is a genuine behavior switch within consumers to move away from traditional decaf methods and onto Swiss Water, which may make Swiss Water an excellent opportunity in the long term if there is a switch in consumer preference and realisation. However, Swiss Water remains somewhat speculative for accelerating growth, as it is already an established brand, having been public for over 2 decades. Hence, I'm rating Swiss Water at a 'Hold'.

The Different Decaffeinating Processes

The most common method remains the solvent method, which can be done directly or indirectly.

For the direct solvent method, beans are steamed for around 30 minutes so they are prime for extraction as the pores open up. They are then rinsed with either methylene chloride or ethyl acetate for about 10 hours. The chemicals are then removed with extremely high steam, as the steam is essentially blasted onto the coffee beans as it starts to remove the chemicals. This is very common as the method is cheap, which also results in cheaper coffee beans, but it is also known as the worst method since it removes the flavour of the coffee. What this basically means is the low price reflects the quality/flavour, a lower price leads to more flavourless decaf coffee. This essentially differentiates from the idea that other methods keep the flavour better and give a more premium taste and feeling.

The indirect solvent method boils the coffee beans with water so the water collects the flavour and components from the beans, which is then mixed with either methylene chloride or ethyl acetate so the solvent extracts the caffeine from the liquid rather than directly from the beans themselves. The water is then boiled again to evaporate the solvent alongside the caffeine, which is then mixed back with the beans to reabsorb the coffee.

Those are the methods that include using chemical solvents, and the other 2 main decaffeination methods don't involve using any chemical solvents.

The CO2 decaffeination process soaks the green coffee beans in highly compressed liquid CO2, where the CO2 selectively only draws out caffeine from the coffee bean.

The last method is the Swiss Water Process, also known as water decaf coffee (since Swiss Water is just the brand name), puts the green coffee beans in hot water to dissolve the caffeine. However, since the hot water would also dissolve other components like sugar, the resulting mixture is run through a filter, Swiss Water uses a proprietary carbon filter system, until there is no more than 0.1% of caffeine remaining in it. The remaining liquid, or what they call GCE (Green Coffee Extract), is then soaked back into the batch of green coffee beans, which leaves the other components and excludes the caffeine, at least most of it anyway.

The main difference between the CO2 process and the Swiss Water process is that it depends on the quality of the coffee itself. CO2 decaf is more common amongst mass-market coffee, whereas Swiss Water tends to be treated more as a premium product. Comparing both, their target markets tend to be slightly different, but due to the heavy similarities of both, not extracting using chemicals and attempting to only remove the caffeine from the coffee beans themselves, from what I read and reviews, it depends on the consumer to choose which is really better. It would also depend on the roasters as well, as Swiss Water's position isn't the final product; they just produce the decaf beans for roasters to then roast, package and sell to the final consumer. The differentiation of CO2 and Swiss Water isn't heavily differentiated, as Swiss Water is often seen as a premium product.

The Decaf Market

Decaf coffee has been on the rise as consumers are more conscious of what they consume in their bodies, especially with the number of processed foods that go into their bodies every day, which most individuals won't even realise. A report from McKinsey found that 84% of US consumers say wellness is a top or important priority. Nestle's figure was at 82% and mentions that it especially rings true with Gen Z.

Coffee has its pros and cons, such as containing antioxidants, which are beneficial since they have benefits like anti-aging benefits as well as preventing many diseases like cancer and type 2 diabetes according to some studies. One of the main reasons why coffee is consumed is to keep one awake, which could link to headaches, insomnia, anxiety, a faster heart rate and many other health issues.

Globally, coffee is deeply embedded into diets, so rather than consuming regular coffee, decaf coffee rises for individuals who want the taste and flavour of coffee but don't want the side effects of coffee, since everyone's tolerance and reaction to coffee is different. Decaf coffee still has some coffee content, but it is way less significant than a regular cup of coffee.

The once demonized decaf continues to be on the rise, as decaf's share of US coffee consumption climbed by 33% YoY from 2024, as Swiss Water is positioned to benefit since it provides a premium decaf method as consumers try to intake less caffeine in their regular diets. Decaf continues to grow, with projections from 2025 to 2030 at a CAGR of 6.58%.

Swiss Water's Moat Evaluation

When it comes to evaluating moat, the key aspects I focus on are the ability to scale and economies of scale, intangible assets, switching costs, and barriers to entry. The reason why I even thought of Swiss Water having a moat despite obvious huge competition is due to their intangible assets, which is why I want to frame a bigger and better picture of their moat. I'm going to compare 2 different aspects, one to the moat of the specific decaf method and one to the decaf market in general.

I'm going to start with intangible assets, as Swiss Water has massive intangible assets compared to its peers, as whenever the water decaf method is referred to, it directly refers back to Swiss Water's decaf method. Some studies, rather than referring to the water decaf method, would directly refer to the Swiss Water decaf method, since it essentially dominates the market when it comes to this market. The Swiss Water is also patented, so even if a different decaf firm uses similar methods, they can't call it the Swiss Water Process as it is patented. They can probably use similar methods as well as follow how Swiss Water does it, but the brand difference would be significantly different from the Swiss Water name on ready packaged roasted coffee beans with 'Swiss Water Decaf' on it.

Due to this, when it comes to their specific method, there is a huge barrier to entry, especially since Swiss Water is already an established player with relationships with the major coffee roasters globally. However, compared to just the different decaf methods, the barriers to entry are already evidently low since Swiss Water faces significantly high competition as it has already existed for decades. It even has competition from non-solvent methods that don't use chemicals (CO2 method), so the barriers to entry into the decaf market are low since competition is extremely high with no single player really dominating the market.

Now here comes the tricky bit: Swiss Water operates as a B2B (business to business), not a B2C (business to consumer). It accepts green coffee beans from roasters/importers and just decafs the coffee beans as a service, as they themselves don't primarily roast and sell the final coffee product that often has their branding attached to them, as seen above. This is due to their process being favourable amongst consumers and often acting as a sign of premium decaf, but when it comes to their vertical position, they are neither the first nor the last, so they have a unique position where their branding is quite popular amongst decaf coffee, since B2B isn't often on the packaging and B2B doesn't advertise since they don't appeal to customers. Roasters/importers who want to work with Swiss Water will also likely have heard of them, as wanting to roast premium decaf coffee beans as a roaster and not hearing of Swiss Water is simply near impossible for a business. Just typing it Swiss Water in Amazon, a bunch of varieties of decaf coffee shows up, but investors need to remember that they are not the final entity in the vertical supply chain, and they don't need the advertising, but Swiss Water still has that aspect.

Therefore, brand reputation and image are obviously important for Swiss Water, but this aspect doesn't really benefit Swiss Water to an extent, since B2B's don't really advertise, as most roasters already know that Swiss Water exists as a method. Swiss Water is essentially the only decaffeinating coffee process to be on the packaging, so their premium definitely exists as customers give the Swiss Water Process a premium; if not, it simply wouldn't be used on the packaging for the final product, especially the premium where they don't use chemicals to decaf their coffee beans. So horizontally compared to other processes, Swiss Water does have a moat since their process is seen as more premium, whereas the others may be seen as more negative, which is the main differentiation of no chemical decaf and chemical decaf when it comes to being advertised on the packaging of the final product.

When it comes to efficiency, scale, and economies of scale, there isn't one to compare Swiss Water to since they don't really have any competition with their specific methods. Decaf from its core is already kind of flawed since it is adding an extra process to coffee, so it is essentially a duplicate, worse version of coffee without caffeine content, which is one of the main reasons why coffee is consumed in the first place anyway. Swiss Water's process is a premium to already a premium process, so the margins will likely be lower than its peers. There isn't any clear public information regarding other pure decaf processing margins, but as seen above with gross and operating margins already at rather low levels, with gross margins contracting YoY and now at 10.3%, this means it also doesn't have a moat in this aspect.

When it comes to switching costs, the switching cost of perhaps having the Swiss Water brand on the packaging as well as using the water-decaf method may be high, as the Swiss Water Process essentially dominates this decaf method, so the switching cost from the water decaf method to the water decaf method is high. However, switching cost to a different decaf is low, since the other methods are perhaps even more common, and there is even a different option to the Swiss Water Process when it comes to a non-chemical process, so the switching cost from this perspective is rather low.

Swiss Water has a moat since it essentially dominates a single type of decaffeination method to exist, especially one that is seen positively by society since it doesn't involve the use of chemicals or harmful products in the decaffeinting process. However, it does not by any means have a moat when it comes to the decaffeinating process. There are other options to choose from when it comes to wanting a non-chemical decaf method, a cheaper decaf method, especially since it appeals as more of a specialty decaf option. This is even more apparent when brands purposefully attach the Swiss Water Decaf label to their packaging, as Swiss Water is also used as a marketing tool for a more premium product. Overall, the moat is honestly rather narrow, as any volatility with coffee will directly affect Swiss Water, as there are other products to Swiss Water, which is the main weakness to the moat when the final product is a B2C despite Swiss Water being a B2B.

Coffee Market and Q3

Now that I have explained why I believe Swiss Water's Moat is rather weak despite having the whole decaffeinating process essentially known to be the Swiss Water Process, it is time to reflect on the quarter, especially with how volatile the quarter has been when it comes to coffee prices due to tariffs.

The top exporter of coffee by a good margin is Brazil, which was heavily affected by tariffs, as consumers have taken a hit. President Trump has removed the 40% tariffs on multiple Brazilian food products, which include green coffee beans, which is primarily one of the reasons for the recent rise of coffee prices. This is after the 20.9% YoY increase in coffee prices as major factors on top of the tariffs, like a lingering drought in Brazil, poor crop conditions in Vietnam and strong demand, continue to affect the coffee supply. The effects of the Trump tariffs were clear on coffee, with American buyers voiding new contracts for Brazilian coffee purchases, with the period from August through October, when the tariffs took effect, seeing Brazilian coffee drop by 52% YoY. The effect has been realised so the tariffs were dropped, as Brazil's crop forecasting agency, Conab, dropped the Arabica coffee crop estimate by -4.9% in early September (same link). Despite the tariffs now being at the baseline of 10%, coffee prices remain elevated YoY as we go into autumn, which is the best season for coffee roasters.

This directly affects Swiss Water as it relates to inventory management by roasters. When coffee prices are high, their margins would get affected more so they would process the coffee beans with Swiss Water when prices are lower, as Swiss Water would in theory benefit more when coffee prices are low since more is going to get processed, while high prices will affect their volumes. Mentioned in their Q3 press release:

Customer demand remained strong, demonstrating the strength of our brand and geographic coverage during a period when supply imbalances have challenged the industry, and importantly, as U.S. grocery channels experience declines in tonnage volume due to rapid retail price increases.

Coffee futures remained volatile throughout the quarter, ending in an elevated position, and while the inverted market and tariffs are causing rapid changes in coffee origin preferences and purchase timing, we benefited from the timing of coffee purchases and favourable foreign exchange movements.

Brazil is the biggest producer of green coffee globally, and all Brazilian coffees processed by Swiss Water and shipped into the US are subject to this elevated tariff. The effect of this change has been a rapid demand shift away from the highest coffee volume producing nation in the world. This is expected to cause short term disruptions and volume issues for the next 3-6 months, and potentially longer.

They clearly mentioned that retail price increases, volatile coffee futures, and Brazilian tariffs are all unfavourable and would cause short-term disruption to operations. A lot of this has been cleared up, but coffee prices remaining elevated is a huge key point to look out for in Q4. On a 6M time scale, the lowest price coffee was during July and August, as the tariffs on August 7th have sent prices up since.

This means that Q3 (July, August, and September) had favourable coffee prices, while prices remained elevated from October onward. Since the prices remain at similar levels after the drop of the Brazilian tariffs, I honestly expect further short-term disruption at these current coffee price levels.

I believe this has already been realised by the market, where roasters are loading up on inventory when prices are low. Zack's estimates for Q3 were at C$44M, whilst revenue came in at C$63M. That's nearly a 50% surprise in their estimates, which is quite a big beat. From the most recent earnings call:

Customers continue to remain cautious with inventory management in response to sustained high coffee futures and ongoing tariff uncertainty. As previously noted, ordering patterns continue to vary through the quarter. Customer mix shifted with importers increasing volumes as they rebuild inventory positions, a change from prior periods when roasters represent a larger share of activity.

Customers continue to remain cautious with inventory management in response to sustained high coffee futures and ongoing tariff uncertainty. As previously noted, ordering patterns continue to vary through the quarter. Customer mix shifted with importers increasing volumes as they rebuild inventory positions, a change from prior periods when roasters represent a larger share of activity.

This essentially reinforces the idea that Swiss Water will be affected by this exact factor, that importers/roasters are building inventory. As the next quarter is expected to be similar to the prior year at C$49M, which is still above Zack's estimates but is slightly disappointing considering the growth from the first 3 quarters. With coffee prices remaining at high since the earnings call, it is clear that Q4 will be negatively affected, with the main question being how much, which can be more accurately assessed in the next quarter.

Valuation

Valuation remains a tricky aspect to judge Swiss Water, especially since there isn't really a company to compare Swiss Water to. On top of that, the volume and liquidity are extremely low, with 9.54M outstanding shares and the average volume in the last 10 days being only at 1.15k. They are also currently positive in net income, compared to being negative in the prior year.

Despite revenue soaring, gross profits are flat, while operating income actually fell YoY despite 50% growth in revenue in Q3. The positive net income was hugely contributed by non-operating items, which is most likely due to hedging. From the earnings call:

Q3 net income is $216,000 compared to a net loss of $791,000 in Q3 2024. Aside from the factors we discussed previously, Q3's increase in nonoperating or other income and expenses is driven by a $728,000 loss on risk management activities, primarily related the timing of rolling hedge contracts forward in a persistently inverted NY'C' market. This timing difference between recognizing the cost of rolling positions and the recovery of those costs through customer invoicing results in incremental realized losses during the quarter.

As Frank mentioned, we continue to price for the cost of the inversion consistent with the industry practice and expect these costs to be covered through customer collections over the coming months and into 2026. We also recorded mark-to-market adjustments reflecting commodity price fluctuations and the U.S. dollar strength. These results are consistent with our structured approach to managing pricing volatility, mitigating risk exposure and maintaining alignment with our supply commitments.

From what I can reflect on, the reason for the positive net income was due to their coffee hedges, which swung in their favour, which essentially isn't part of their core business, being in non-operating income and other for a reason. This essentially signals that net income in this quarter may be positive, but gross and operating margins, which are more long-term signals for Swiss Water, are actually getting contracted and even being flat/negative YoY with 50% revenue growth. The volatility is often short-lived, so remain cautious since the positive net income will also likely be short-lived.

They also have a debt problem, with debt being at multiples of their market cap, with cash just under C$4M. With positive net income for now, the next quarter has to be heavily monitored to understand a better picture of how long the cash they have can last before another potential dilution to raise cash, which may not be far away if margins don't improve in the next quarter, since the recent positive net income is very much short-term.

If Q4 revenues come in at C$49M, that would bring the full-year revenue to C$241.714M for 2025. That would be a trailing 12-month P/S of 0.176, which is honestly quite cheap, if the investor believes that Swiss Water will have an inflection point with better margins and break even with their core business rather than some short-term hedge. Their revenue growth soaring is impressive, but no matter how much the top line improves, the poor performance with the bottom line needs to also improve, as at the end of the day, the amount left after operating expenses is way more important than revenue growth, especially when revenue growth is 50% YoY whilst operating income fell 20% YoY in Q3.

Final Thoughts

Swiss Water has a questionable moat despite being seen as the premium specialty decaf coffee and having huge intangible assets, especially since there is another method that doesn't involve chemicals that can also be volume produced. Swiss Water's balance sheet also has to improve, as operating income fell YoY while revenue grew 50% is simply unacceptable when looking at their debt and cash, which in turn leads to low valuations, but maybe too low for a company that provides value. The company has existed for decades, which is the reason I didn't propose a possibility for an acquisition, as it seems unlikely, but a scenario where Swiss Water gets acquired by a major brand could honestly be what investors need. For now, low valuations, a weak balance sheet, and a questionable moat make me remain neutral on Swiss Water, especially when the coffee market continues to be on a downward trend.

data.title: Lock 8% By Horizon Technology Finance's Baby Bonds For Your Ladder Portfolio
data.description:
In today's article, we will look at Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN), its baby bonds, and the opportunities they offer, as well as try to give them a credit rating.

Company Basic Information

  • Total Assets (3rd Quarter): close to $759 million.
  • Current market capitalization: approximately $296 million.
  • Top holdings:

The two most significant parts of HRZN's portfolio are the "Healthcare and pharmaceuticals" industry (47.2%) and "IT Software Services and Equipment" (27.46%).

  • The common stock is currently trading at around a 6% discount:

  • Historical Performance:

The 10-year total return on NAV is 5.21%:

  • 10-year premium/discount chart:

The company's total leverage is around 56.5%. The non-leverage expense ratio is 5.71%, and the gross asset non-leverage expense ratio is 2.33%. It has a 1-year negative z-score of 1.01, as shown in the picture below.

In 2023, Horizon was acquired by Monroe Capital, which gave Horizon access to Monroe’s platform, expertise, and resources. This allowed Horizon to expand its capabilities in sourcing agricultural partners and debt financing for fast-growing companies in the technology, biotechnology, conservation, information services, and sustainability sectors.

Baby Bonds

HRZN has two baby bonds: (HTFC) and (HTFB). Neither is rated by the major credit agencies. Their call dates have passed - they are callable anytime. At the time of writing, HTFB is traded around par, with a yield to worst close to 6.6%, and HTFC is traded below par, with a yield to worst above 8% - that's why it looks more attractive to us. We are facing numerous questions about yield calculations, and we calculate them using the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is considered the most precise method when dealing with ex-dividend dates. Below you can see the different yields to worst vs prices:

The bond pays an annual interest of 6.25%, paid quarterly — $0.390625 each quarter, or $1.5625 per year. The issue was launched on 06/08/2022, in the amount of 2 million bonds, each with a par value of $25. The spread to the 5-year Treasury yield on its IPO date was close to 3.22%, and the spread now stands at 4.52%.

Below is shown a comparison between the baby bonds and their peers with their yields to worst. As you can see, HTFC is among the highest yields to worst in the group:

If we look at the BDC OTC bond universe, we see that HTFC has a close to 1.5% higher yield than the highest-yielding over-the-counter bond:

Credibility

  • Profitability: Here, we get a 3-year average Net Income/Assets of around -3.6%, which corresponds to the worst credit rating of Ca.
  • Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio)- Baa3: The regulatory minimum for BDCs is 150%. HRZN's asset coverage ratio cushion is close to 18%, with an asset coverage ratio of 177%.
  • Asset Quality-Problem loans are close to 2.33%, which is equivalent to a Ba1 credit rating, and 84.89% of assets are senior secured, which is a Baa3 rating.
  • Debt Structure- Debt distributions: Ba1. The secured debt is around 56% of the assets, which equates to a B2 rating.

Final Rating: The weighted average credit score corresponds to "Ba1". But when we adjust that score with a discount to NAV, the historically high IPO yields of the company's bonds, and recent problems in the underlying portfolio, we get an overall adjusted credit score of "Ba2".

Conclusion

HRZN offers two baby bonds that are not rated by the major agencies, but our adjusted credit rating is around Ba2. HTFC stands out with a higher yield compared to its peers – over 8% YTW – which reflects both slightly higher risk and the opportunity for better returns.

data.title: CVS: FY 2026 Margin Recovery, Well Covered Dividends, And Cheap Valuations
data.description:
I previously covered CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) in August 2025, discussing how it had emerged as an outlier during the prior implosion observed in the healthcare insurance sector, as they delivered improved YoY margins despite the rising medical costs.

Combined with the somewhat stable performance metrics observed in the PBM and pharmacy end markets, I had reiterated my Buy rating then, significantly aided by the compelling dividend yields and the rich double-digit upside potential.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CVS stock here, with the raised FY2025 adj EPS guidance and the promising consensus forward estimates well supported by the robust YTD outperformance, the raised premiums/ACA exit, and the four-star award for the majority of its Medicare Advantage plans in 2026.

CVS Delivers Profitable Growth Despite Headwinds

CVS 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, CVS has recorded a further upward recovery along with the wider market by the single digits, with it well outperforming most of its healthcare provider peers in varying degrees.

Much of its outperformance is attributed to their ability to deliver improved YoY profitability despite the industry-wide elevated medical care activity levels, the fee schedule changes, and the aggressive provider coding/billing practices.

At a time when its peers are reporting impacted profit margins, CVS has been able to confidently offer a promising FY2025 adj operating margin guidance of 3.5% at the midpoint (+0.3 points YoY/-2.4 from FY2019 levels of 5.9%) and adj EPS guidance of $6.60 (+21.7% YoY).

These numbers mark a much-needed recovery in its performance metrics after the trough FY2024 performance at 3.2% (-1.6 points YoY) and $5.42 (-37.9% YoY), respectively.

When comparing CVS' expanding operating margins of 3.3% (+0.6 points YoY) and moderating Medical Benefit Ratio [MBR] of 92.8% in FQ3'25 (-2.4 points YoY/+8.6 from FY2019 levels of 84.2%) to its direct peers' recent performance, including:

  • UnitedHealth (UNH) at operating margins of 3.7% (-4.9 points YoY) and MBR of 89.9% (+4.7 points YoY) in FQ3'25,
  • Centene Corporation (CNC) at adj operating margins of 0.4% (-1.1 points YoY) and HBR of 92.7% (+3.5 points YoY) in FQ3'25, and
  • Humana (HUM) at operating margins of 1.2% (-1.2 points YoY) and HBR of 91.1% (+1.3 points YoY) in FQ3'25, respectively,

it is apparent that CVS' ability to drive expanded profitable growth cadence without being overly affected by the industry wide issues has been extremely impressive.

Most importantly, CVS recently reported that "more than 81% of Aetna Medicare Advantage members are in 2026 Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plans that are rated 4 stars or higher (out of 5 stars) by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. Additionally, more than 63% of Aetna Medicare Advantage members are in a 4.5-star plan for 2026."

This is a boon indeed, since it may allow the healthcare provider to receive higher annual bonus payments while offsetting some of their elevated medical care costs.

This is on top of CVS already looking to reprice "approximately half of our group MA revenue in 2026," allowing them to drive "meaningful margin improvement at Aetna" aided by the upcoming exit from the individual exchange business (also known as the Affordable Care Act).

As a result of these developments, I am of the opinion that the healthcare provider is likely to report a notable moderation in its MBR metric and a further expansion in its operating margins from FY2026 onwards, as similarly highlighted by the management in the FQ2'25 earnings call:

These (group MA) contracts are typically 3- to 5-year contracts. And so we are taking a very disciplined approach to renewing the business also as we consider new business, and we are contemplating the elevated trends as we go through that process with them...

The absolute objective is to write the business, so it achieves target margin. But sometimes it takes more than 1 cycle to get there. (Seeking Alpha)

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Based on the YTD adj EPS outperformance at $5.66 (+33.8% YoY), I believe that CVS is likely to exceed the recently raised FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $6.60 (+21.7% YoY) as well.

Otherwise, there is no denying that the current consensus forward estimates are already quite promising, with the company expected to deliver an accelerated recovery at a top/bottom-line CAGR of +5.7%/+14.2% through FY2027.

I am of the opinion that the consensus may also upgrade their forward estimates from current levels, assuming a further expansion in CVS' profit margins as their plan repricing kicks in from FY2026 onwards and flows through their top/bottom-line performances.

With the stock's sustained bullish support and the speculative breakout from the 2Y resistance levels of $80s hinging on its margin recovery prospects, I urge readers to closely monitor their upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in February 2026 and the management's initial FY2026 guidance.

CVS Remains A Great Buy

CVS 10Y Stock Price

For now, CVS has enjoyed a rich +72.1% rally since the January 2025 bottom, albeit with the stock failing to break out of the 2Y resistance levels of $80s by mid October 2025 as the stock also consolidates near to the 50 day moving averages by the time of writing.

CVS Technical Indicators

Thanks to the recent sideways trading, I am of the opinion that CVS remains attractive here, with the elevated trading volumes well balanced by the moderate RSIs - implying a further consolidation at current levels pending further catalysts.

CVS Valuations

For now, given the nascency of their margin recovery process, I am of the opinion that CVS remains rather cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.02x and my estimated FY2027 P/E of 9.29x, based on the current stock prices of $75 and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $8.07.

Most importantly, the healthcare provider remains extremely profitable on a cash flow basis at a YTD sum of $7.24B (inline YoY), allowing them to service the YTD dividend obligations of $2.55B (inline YoY) while maintaining a healthy balance sheet.

The discounted P/E valuations against the 5Y mean of 10.20x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.34x are also why CVS now boasts a richer forward dividend yield of 3.43% compared to the 5Y mean of 2.91% and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 2.46%.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS of $8.07 and the 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 13.34x (cheap against its peer group 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 16.63x), there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +43.4% to my long-term price target of $107.60 as well.

As a result of the robust dual pronged return prospects across capital appreciation/dividend income, the promising margin recovery from FY2026 onwards, and the bullish support observed in the recent stock price movement/technical indicators, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CVS stock here.

data.title: Why An Investment Process Matters, Especially In Volatile Markets
data.description:
Markets have had a strong run year-to-date. But with those highs come some familiar concerns, such as valuations, volatility, and whether the rally can keep going. Benjamin Gossack, Managing Director, Co-Head of Global Equity Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management, discusses the benefits of having a consistent investment process, even if markets face uncertainty.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - Markets have had a strong run year to date, with the S&P 500 up about 16%. The NASDAQ up about 20%. But with those highs come some familiar concerns -- valuations, volatility, and whether the rally can keep going. My next guest says markets have already seen a big sell-off. And all the uncertainty out there highlights the importance of sticking to your investment process. Ben Gossack is with TD Asset Management, and he joins me now. It is lovely to have you here.

Benjamin Gossack - Yeah, it's a pleasure. Thank you for having me.

Kim Parlee - I feel like that -- what I just said, which came from the feedback you gave my producer, was directed to me directly because I know I get scared off. When you see markets so high, you just think it's too much. It's too much. So maybe just tell us a bit of what you're hearing about why people are feeling so uneasy about these market highs.

Benjamin Gossack - Yeah, it seems if we're at new highs, how could we go higher? We can only retrace what happened. And that seems to be what consistently is coming up in conversations as I go across Canada. But how are we supposed to get to higher highs once we're at these levels? So I think so much has happened this year. It's just been a barrage of news and various themes.

And I think people forget that we already had a 20% drawdown this year, and meanwhile everyone's expecting and waiting for the next shoe to fall. And we've cleared out a lot of concern in terms of policy back in spring.

Kim Parlee - Let's take a look at the chart. As always, thank you. You bring us lots of fascinating charts to look at. So this is a look at the S&P 500, the drawdown. And yeah, to your point, there has been a drawdown.

Benjamin Gossack - There was a major drawdown. So it took 33 days, so about a month, for the market to swiftly fall 21% and then about 56 days for us to recover that high. We did have a bit of a wobble within the past two weeks where we did fall about 5%, and there were so many different narratives as to what was driving that. And NVIDIA (NVDA) delivered record earnings, but then the market sold off. So was that the end of AI, and is it a bubble? And yet now we're probably 1% off those highs.

But again, if we did have a massive drawdown in a swift period because there was uncertainty about policy, and when markets got some clarity about what trade and how this administration was going to go about it, we did rally through that. But I think it's just that people have already forgotten that. And now we're waiting, "Well, if we're at highs, then...

Kim Parlee - It's going to happen again.

Benjamin Gossack - ...what's going to be the next pothole for the market?"

Kim Parlee - Let me ask you, you were saying that you believe that some of the pullbacks we've seen weren't driven by fundamentals, to your point, but you're saying these are our technical dynamics. Is this just the concerns about policy or other just uncertainty out there?

Benjamin Gossack - So I think markets are complicated because we have a whole bunch of people expressing their opinions, and then it all gets merged together, and we get a clearing price, and that's the price of a stock. And it changes every second throughout the day. We do spend a part of our process. We love fundamentals and secular trends and understanding human behaviors. But an equal part of that process is understanding patterns. And one pattern that we keep seeing, especially in this instance of the market, is we're seeing a double-top pattern.

So a stock or a sector had made a high. It had come off. It attempted to make another high and rolled over, or it broke out, and it failed to hold the breakout. And so people, when they see that pattern, they're like, "Oh, we're ready to go. Let's get all of our purchases going in." And then when that pattern doesn't hold, everyone's caught on the wrong side. And the outcome is a very vicious sell-off.

Kim Parlee - Can we bring up -- because I think, again, you talk about failed breakouts. We have a chart that shows that. So maybe just let's bring it up and talk a bit about what this is.

Benjamin Gossack - Right. So obviously, the key topic in the market is AI. And do we have a bubble? And is it NVIDIA's chip? Is it Google's chip? A whole bunch of stories. What people are looking at right here is the technology sector of the S&P 500. And I've taken that sector, and I've divided it by the market. All that people need to understand about this chart is if the chart is going up, it means that technology has relative strength to the market. And if it's going down, it means that technology has relative weakness to the market.

And so we have this red line. So that identifies the previous high, which happened in 2024. Tech did actually underperform for almost 10 months. Most people did not notice that. And then we actually rallied through and surpassed last year's relative high. But we failed. And so we had this vicious sell-off.

It does coincide conveniently with NVIDIA delivering earnings, but that wasn't good enough for the market. And that allowed a whole host of bearish opinions to sound really credible. And they could be credible. But it's the viciousness, I think, of a failed breakout that makes those bearish tones sound very credible. But yet it could have easily just been a technical sell-off, Kim.

Kim Parlee - It's interesting, too, because we talk about discipline investment process. And this isn't motherhood. This is about sometimes understanding your own behavior and how you get in your own way.

Benjamin Gossack - Yes, I do think prices drive sentiment. When I first started in this industry, I used to think there was a catalyst. You'd try to figure out what's driving the stock, and that would drive the behavior behind a stock movement. But really, I do think prices move. And then we as humans start to build the rationale for why it happened and how we feel about that.

And with social media and the speed at which I'm seeing the markets move faster and faster, one week, we're talking, it's got to be this, and the next week, we're flipping, and we're saying it's got to be this. And I think for the average person, that sounds very contradictory. It sounds confusing.

And this is why we really do preach having a process. How is it that you go about managing money? How do you think that you create value with your process? Because right now, we're just getting bombarded from all sides, and that process will at least -- it's sort of that compass where you're like, okay, I don't know where we are. We're in the middle of the ocean. But that compass helps guide you through, because no one is really trying to help you make money. They're going to try to confuse you as much as possible.

Kim Parlee - There is no shortage of AI information out there. I feel as though I'm almost drowning in it all the time to understand what's out there. What's your take on it from your perspective? What do you pay attention to?

Benjamin Gossack - So there's a lot of takes. There's a lot of people providing content. They think AI is going to do this or do that. I've tried to go more glass half full. Who would have imagined we'd be going into strangers' cars in 1990, 2000 as we go through the tech bubble?

So the iPhone doesn't come until 2007, and that wasn't discussed back then. So I think AI can do a lot of things. And I've just been taking it one day at a time in terms of how do I really think it's going to change the world, but it is having implications today in terms of stock prices and valuations.

Kim Parlee - Okay, so let's talk about that. There's been lots of different ways it does. But you've brought four charts of what's catching your attention. And one of these is you talk about how AI is reshaping the software world. How?

Benjamin Gossack - So everyone's focused on who has the best chip and who's got the best accelerator and who's got the best fiber optic cables. And what people may not be noticing is that software has been a casualty of this AI trend. For the longest time -- I used to cover technology starting in 2013. There was this moniker that software eats the world.

So we may have a breakthrough in hardware. So there was an iPhone. There's the desktop computer. But all the profits ended up with the software companies because they developed the interface that provided us value. Office suites provided productivity to the enterprise. And so we may not know exactly what AI does, but we do know it codes very well.

And so if it can code me tax accounting software, well, do I need to pay all those subscriptions to the major tax accounting software companies or legal software or customer relationship management? And so right now, the market doesn't know who the winner is and who's going to survive. And so they've decided to step away from the software sector.

Kim Parlee - And that's what we're seeing here in this chart; just this fall to the right is just to say, we're just not sure.

Benjamin Gossack - Right. So this chart is basically a tug of war between software and the S&P 500. And you see this rollover, which means it's -- imagine the S&P 500 just really pulling software to the other side and just saying, "We are not comfortable being in this space." And that, I think, is a big paradigm shift because it's been very easy from a technology investor perspective to just own software companies and avoid hardware.

Kim Parlee - Yeah, it's sobering. Okay, the other thing that's been happening, of course, is there's been so much emphasis on the Mag Seven and chips and NVIDIA, as you mentioned, and power, which, of course, is powering AI. And now there's the emergence of the anti-AI trade. So what is that?

Benjamin Gossack - So yes, I think there has been too much emphasis on the Mag Seven, and people have not counted other stocks that have outperformed. But as you said, yes, it's about power. It's about industrials. So my biggest challenge in terms of managing a US portfolio is I don't want to have a whole bunch of holdings, but they're all tied to one idea, which right now is AI.

And AI, like you said. In the past, if I wanted to get defensive, I'd go buy a utility. Well, right now, that utility is all about powering the data center. So the old paradigm is actually going to introduce more risk when I start reaching out for these sectors that would have helped me in the past.

What we are starting to see emerge right now is, yes, the market likes AI. It's exciting. But it also can introduce a lot of risk. So how do you properly add some defense to your portfolio? And we're starting to see emerge certain sectors, certain stocks where we can actually hedge that AI bet in that theme.

Kim Parlee - Let's bring up a few. The first one is health care.

Benjamin Gossack - Yeah, so health care has been a very challenging sector. So again, if we go back to our tug-of-war example, healthcare has lost the tug of war to the S&P 500. This is XLV. It's an ETF that represents the health care sector. So you have pharma, MedTech, drug distributors. They've just been losing. But we're starting to see that move because, well, there isn't a lot of AI right now in health care.

So we're seeing pharma companies move. We're seeing medical distribution companies. It's bringing back some life to this sector that most people had -- they had a lot of exposure to it, but because it's underperformed, they have really reduced their exposure. So there's a lot of opportunity here. And the one nice thing about health care -- it's not like -- I like banks. It doesn't matter really what bank I buy. They're all going to swim in the same direction. Every health care company is different.

So we're seeing a move. You still have to dig in and find the right companies, but it provides some diversity for the portfolio, which is very exciting right now.

Kim Parlee - Yeah, it is important and hard to find. We've only got about 30 seconds. So I want to make sure I get -- the next one is biotech. So I assume the same principle here.

Benjamin Gossack - Same principle, but even harder. So they're all moving. But you really have to know your biotech company. Because if you miss a phase trial, it can be disastrous.

Kim Parlee - Yeah. And then finally, utilities. But interestingly, these are not North American utilities. As you mentioned, these are EU utilities.

Benjamin Gossack - Yes, so North America is all about powering the data center, whether it's going to be nuclear or gas. In Europe, that's not even part of the discussion right now. So I'd say the European utilities are still acting like those traditional utilities that most investors would be familiar with when they're wanting to add some defense to their portfolio.

Original Post
